title: Apply: The Fourth Amendment
description: Apply your knowledge of the Fourth Amendment to this set of multiple-choice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Otis’s 1761 speech, “Against [[☃ definition 1]].”**

Every one with this writ may be a tyrant; if this [[☃ definition 2]] be legal, a tyrant in a legal manner also may control, imprison, or murder any one within the realm. . . . a person with this writ, in the daytime, may enter all houses, shops . . .  at will, and command all to assist him. . . . Now one of the most essential branches of English liberty is the freedom of one’s house. A man’s house is his castle; and whilst he is quiet, he is as well guarded as a prince in his castle. This writ, if it should be declared legal, would totally [[☃ definition 3]] this privilege. . . .

**Which statement best summarizes Otis’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: court orders that require members of law enforcement to perform certain tasks

choices.content: Englishmen have more privacy than colonists, as Englishmen are more likely to live in castles.
Writs of assistance can help protect people from tyranny when used correctly by the British government.
People have a reasonable expectation of privacy, and writs of assistance violate that right.
Privacy is a natural right and the government can never infringe on that right, even with a lawful search warrant.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Olmstead v. United States* (1928).**

The [Fourth] Amendment itself shows that the search is to be of material things—the person, the house, his papers, or his [[☃ definition 1]]. The description of the warrant necessary to make the proceeding lawful is that it must specify the place to be searched and the person or things to be seized. . . .

The Amendment does not forbid what was done here. There was no searching. There was no seizure. The evidence was secured by the use of the sense of hearing, and that only. There was no entry of the houses or offices of the defendants. . . .

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Fourth Amendment protects people against  unreasonable physical searches and all other kinds of surveillance and information gathering.
The Fourth Amendment does not require police officers to get a warrant before searching a person suspected of a crime.
The Fourth Amendment requires the police to issue a warrant for any evidence introduced in a trial.
The Fourth Amendment protects only against physical search and seizure, not against conversations that have been overheard.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Olmstead v. United States* (1928).**

The [Fourth] Amendment itself shows that the search is to be of material things—the person, the house, his papers, or his [[☃ definition 1]]. The description of the warrant necessary to make the proceeding lawful is that it must specify the place to be searched and the person or things to be seized. . . .

The Amendment does not forbid what was done here. There was no searching. There was no seizure. The evidence was secured by the use of the sense of hearing, and that only. There was no entry of the houses or offices of the defendants. . . .

**Which case would overturn the reasoning in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Mapp v. Ohio* (1961)
*Katz v. United States* (1967)
*Terry v. Ohio* (1968)
*McDonald v. City of Chicago* (2012)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Justice Harlan’s concurring opinion in *Katz v. United States* (1967).**

My understanding of the rule that has emerged from prior decisions is that there is a twofold requirement, first that a person have exhibited an actual ([[☃ definition 1]]) expectation of privacy and, second, that the expectation be one that society is prepared to recognize as ‘reasonable.’ . . .

The critical fact in this case is that ‘(o)ne who occupies it, (a telephone booth) shuts the door behind him, and pays the toll that permits him to place a call is surely entitled to assume’ that his conversation is not being intercepted. The point is not that the booth is ‘accessible to the public’ at other times, but that it is a temporarily private place whose momentary occupants’ expectations of freedom from [[☃ definition 2]] are recognized as reasonable. . .

**Which statement best summarizes Justice Harlan’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: based on one's own feelings or opinions
the act of entering a private place

choices.content: The Fourth Amendment protects communication when there is a reasonable expectation of privacy.
The Fourth Amendment does not prohibit the government from getting information by monitoring electronic communications.
The Fourth Amendment protects against wiretapping of personal phones, but not against wiretapping calls in public places.
The Fourth Amendment protects people only from unreasonable searches and seizures of material items, and conversations are not material.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Justice Harlan’s concurring opinion in *Katz v. United States* (1967).**

My understanding of the rule that has emerged from prior decisions is that there is a twofold requirement, first that a person have exhibited an actual ([[☃ definition 1]]) expectation of privacy and, second, that the expectation be one that society is prepared to recognize as ‘reasonable.’ . . .

The critical fact in this case is that ‘(o)ne who occupies it, (a telephone booth) shuts the door behind him, and pays the toll that permits him to place a call is surely entitled to assume’ that his conversation is not being intercepted. The point is not that the booth is ‘accessible to the public’ at other times, but that it is a temporarily private place whose momentary occupants’ expectations of freedom from [[☃ definition 2]] are recognized as reasonable. . .

**Based on the reasoning in the excerpt, which scenario would be an example of the government violating the Fourth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: based on one's own feelings or opinions
the act of entering a private place

choices.content: A police officer arrests a couple after he overhears a woman in a restaurant admit to robbing a bank with her boyfriend.
A police officer identifies a suspect by reviewing the footage from a red light camera and arrests them.
A police officer arrests a man after she looks through a shop window and sees him physically threatening a store clerk.
A police officer places a listening device in a rental car that belongs to a person she suspects is bribing political officials.

assessment_items.question_content:
Calvin is driving down the highway when he sees a police car flashing its lights in his rearview mirror. He pulls over, and when the officer asks to see his license and registration, he [[☃ definition 1]]. After the officer confirms that Calvin owns the car and that it’s registered with the state, she asks Calvin a series of questions. She asks about his plans for the night, where he was coming from and whether he has anything illegal in the car, like drugs or open bottles of alcohol. Finally the officer asks, “Would you mind if I search your vehicle?” Calvin says, “Sure. Go ahead.” The officer then conducts a search of Calvin’s vehicle for the next thirty minutes, though she does not have a warrant for the search. Finally, the officer lets Calvin go with a warning to slow down as he drives. 

**What type of exception to the warrant requirement does this scenario demonstrate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Exigent circumstances
Consent
Plain view
Exclusionary rule

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Mapp v. Ohio* (1961).**

We hold that all evidence obtained by searches and seizures in violation of the Constitution is, by that same authority, inadmissible in a state court. . . .

Having once recognized that the right to privacy embodied in the Fourth Amendment is enforceable against the States, and that the right to be secure against rude invasions of privacy by state officers is, therefore, constitutional in origin, we can no longer permit that right to remain an empty promise. Because it is enforceable in the same manner and to like effect as other basic rights secured by the Due Process Clause, we can no longer permit it to be [[☃ definition 1]] at the [[☃ definition 2]] of any police officer who, in the name of law enforcement itself, chooses to suspend its enjoyment. 

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Any evidence that was taken without a warrant generally cannot be used in state courts, as the Fourth Amendment applies to the states.
Courts can use only physical material as evidence in a court case, as other types of evidence can be manipulated.
Law enforcement can seize a person’s property only if they have a warrant signed by a judge.
The Fourth Amendment applies only to the federal government, not to state or local law enforcement.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which scenario is an example of evidence that would likely be excluded from a trial through the exclusionary rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A woman turns in a weapon to the police and tells them she thinks her father used it to commit a crime.
A police officer searches a man who is under arrest for fraud and finds drugs in his pocket.
A man is detained by a police officer during a protest, and the police officer takes his phone to identify other  protesters.
A police officer stops a car for a broken tail light and arrests the driver after seeing open containers of alcohol in the car.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Terry v. Ohio* (1961).**

If this case involved police conduct subject to the Warrant Clause of the Fourth Amendment, we would have to [[☃ definition 1]] whether “probable cause” existed to justify the search and seizure which took place. However, that is not the case. . . . [W]e deal here with an entire [[☃ definition 2]] of police conduct—necessarily swift action [[☃ definition 3]] the on-the-spot observations of the officer on the beat—which historically has not been, and as a practical matter could not be, subjected to the warrant procedure. Instead, the conduct involved in this case must be tested by the Fourth Amendment’s general [[☃ definition 4]] against unreasonable searches and seizures. . . .

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: official prohibition; the act of banning something

choices.content: Stop-and-frisk fails to meet the Fourth Amendment’s general “reasonableness” standard, as it involves suspicion of criminal behavior without proof.
Stop-and-frisk is judged by the Fourth Amendment’s general “reasonableness” standard because it involves instant police action.
Stop-and-frisk fails to meet the Fourth Amendment’s strict “probable cause” requirement, making it unlawful without a warrant.
Stop-and-frisk is a part of typical police behavior and does not need to meet any of the search and seizure requirements in the Fourth Amendment.

assessment_items.question_content:
Police officers receive a call that there is a disturbance at a home. When police arrive, they hear someone yell “Help!” from inside the house. After hearing the cry, the officers break down the locked front door and enter the home without a warrant. 

**This scenario is an example of what type of exception to the warrant requirement?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Exigent circumstances
Consent
Plain view
Exclusionary rule

assessment_items.question_content:
**A group of Boston citizens formed a citizens’ committee led by Samuel Adams, James Otis, and Joseph Warren to oppose the British government’s control over the American colonies. This is an excerpt from the Boston’s Committee of Correspondence Report on November 2, 1772. In the report, the committee listed twelve violations of colonial rights by the British government.**

Each of these [British] petty officers so made is entrusted with Power more absolute and [[☃ definition 1]] than ought to be lodged in the hands of any Man or body of Men whatsoever. . . . Thus our Houses, and even our Bed-Chambers, are exposed to be [[☃ definition 2]], our Boxes, Trunks and Chests broke open, ravaged and plundered, by Wretches, whom no [[☃ definition 3]] Man would venture to employ even as Menial Servants; whenever they are pleased to say they suspect there are in the House, Wares, etc. for which the [[☃ definition 4]] have not been paid. 

[[☃ definition 5]] instances of the [[☃ definition 6]] exercise of this Power, have frequently happened in this and other seaport Towns. . . . These Officers may under the color of Law and the cloak of a general warrant, break through the sacred Rights of the [[☃ definition 7]], ransack Men’s Houses, destroy their Securities, carry off their Property, and with little Danger to themselves commit the most horrid Murders.

**This excerpt serves as evidence of which development during the colonial period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The British military’s forced and arbitrary occupation of colonial homes
The British government’s employment of menial servants to represent the colonists in Parliament
The British government’s widespread use of taxes and duties to punish colonial resistance
The British Crown’s use of general warrants to target political enemies

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the transcript of the English case *Wilkes v. Wood* (1763).**

The Lord Chief Justice . . . observed, that the present cause chiefly turned upon the general question, whether a Secretary of State has a power to force persons’ houses, break open their locks, seize their papers, &c. upon a bare suspicion of a [[☃ definition 1]] by a [[☃ definition 2]], without name of the person charged. 

A strange question, to be [[☃ definition 4]] in these days, when the constitution is so well fixed, when we have a prince upon the throne, whose virtues are so great and [[☃ definition 3]], and whose regard for the [[☃ definition 5]] is such, that he must frown at every [[☃ definition 6]] upon their liberty. Nothing can be more unjust in itself, than that the proof of a man's guilt shall be extracted from his own bosom. No legal authority, in the present case, to justify the action. No [[☃ definition 7]], no legal determinations, not an Act of Parliament itself, is sufficient to warrant any [[☃ definition 8]] contrary to the spirit of the constitution.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a published false statement that damages a person’s reputation
an order to search that gives law enforcement broad authority to search without specifying who or what to search for
person is ruled by a king or queen
legal decisions that influence later decisions

choices.content: The court cannot use any evidence obtained during an illegal search to convict a person of a crime.
Any order to search a person’s property is unlawful, as every person is guaranteed total privacy.
Warrants that do not specify the terms of a search are unlawful and violate people’s liberty.
The government has the authority to search any person if that person is suspected of a crime.
